title: Mille-feuille
subtitle: Three layers of puff pastry sandwiched with crème pâtissière
excerpt: The French napoleon: three flat layers of all-butter puff pastry baked between trays for a uniform shatter, sandwiched with crème pâtissière, finished with a feathered icing top. One 30 cm slab, serving eight.
sourceNotes: Marie-Antoine Carême, L'Art de la cuisine française (1833), Project Gutenberg: documents the early mille-feuille in classical French pâtisserie. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903): documents the napoleon form with the feathered chocolate-and-fondant top. Crème pâtissière method is documented across pre-1928 French sources.

recipe.freezeNotes: The cream layer weeps and the pastry softens; this is a same-day pastry.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the puff slabs the day before; make the crème pâtissière the day before; assemble within 2 hours of serving.

recipeTools.notes: Two trays for sandwiching the puff.

glossaryTerms.definition: Puff pastry baked between two flat trays with weight on top stays flat as it bakes — the layers build inside but the top stays level. Ideal for mille-feuille where the three slabs need to stack evenly. Roll the puff thin (2 mm), lay between baking paper on a tray, set a second tray and a heavy oven-safe weight on top, and bake. The result is shatter-crisp, even-edged, and shows the layers clearly when cut.

A thick cooked custard of milk, sugar, egg yolks and cornflour, flavoured with vanilla. The hot milk is tempered into the yolk mixture, then returned to the pan and cooked until thick and glossy. Cooled and beaten smooth, it pipes cleanly and holds its shape between pastry layers.

content.text: Mille-feuille (a thousand sheets, also called a napoleon) is three flat slabs of puff pastry sandwiched with crème pâtissière. The puff is baked between two trays so it stays flat and shatter-crisp; the crème pâtissière is cooked on the hob and piped between layers; the top is finished with a thin white-icing glaze, fed across with thin lines of dark chocolate and dragged with a skewer to feather.

Best assembled within an hour of serving; the cream softens the puff on standing. Bought all-butter puff pastry simplifies the recipe — making puff from scratch is its own project. Look for puff with at least 80% butter on the label.

items.prepNote: split, seeds scraped — or 2 teaspoons vanilla bean paste

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Roll the {{puff-pastry}} on a floured surface to a 30×40 cm rectangle, 2 mm thick. Lay onto a tray lined with baking paper. Cover with another sheet of baking paper and a second tray on top, weighted with an oven-safe weight. Bake for 20 minutes; remove the top tray and weight, scatter the first 30 g of {{caster-sugar}} over the puff, and bake another 8–10 minutes; the puff should be deep gold and the sugar should caramelise into a thin glaze. Cool fully on a rack.

Heat the {{whole-milk}} with the seeds and split pod of the {{vanilla-pod}} in a saucepan until just steaming. Whisk the {{egg-yolks}} with the second {{caster-sugar}} and {{cornflour}} until pale. Pour the warm milk into the yolks in a slow stream, whisking. Return to the pan and cook over a medium heat, whisking constantly, until the mixture thickens and bubbles a few times. Take off the heat. Whisk in the third {{unsalted-butter}}. Pour into a shallow dish, cover the surface with cling film, and chill for at least an hour.

Cut the cooled puff slab into three equal rectangles. Beat the chilled crème pâtissière smooth with a whisk, then pipe half across the first slab. Lay the second slab on top, gently. Pipe the remaining crème over and lay the third slab on top, sugar-glazed side up. Whisk the {{icing-sugar}} with the second {{water}} to a thick pourable glaze. Spread evenly over the top of the mille-feuille. Pipe thin lines of melted {{dark-chocolate}} across the icing along the long axis. Drag a skewer through the lines crosswise to feather. Chill an hour; cut with a long sharp knife in clean slow strokes.
